nativeRange.habitat: The Eyespot Gourami inhabits small, muddy, shallow pools and slow-moving streams, as well as well-vegetated lake margins with leaf litter, submerged branches, and dense aquatic plants. Its natural waters are oxygen-poor and heavily shaded, found across the upper Ayeyarwady basin and around Lake Indawgyi in northern Myanmar.

decorPreferences: dense planting with fine-leaved and floating plants
driftwood roots and branches for cover

diet.notes: In the wild the Eyespot Gourami is a micropredator, feeding on small aquatic crustaceans, worm larvae, and insect larvae. In captivity it requires daily offerings of appropriately sized live or frozen foods — Artemia nauplii, Daphnia, micro-worms, grindal worms, and Cyclops are all suitable options. Newly imported specimens frequently refuse dried or prepared foods, and weaning them onto high-quality frozen alternatives takes patience. A varied live-food diet is strongly recommended for long-term health and for conditioning fish ahead of breeding attempts.

avoidWith: fish large enough to predate on them

imageCandidates.notes: Andrew Bogott; 1149x599. Sharp, well-lit live specimen showing whole fish with golden lateral sheen and chocolate mottling; best-conditioned in-colour individual.
Andrew Bogott; 1391x806. Crisp full-body side view showing the chocolate-brown 'Burmese chocolate gourami' coloration and prominent eye; complementary angle.

summary: The Eyespot Gourami (Parasphaerichthys ocellatus) is a rarely imported labyrinth fish from Myanmar, seldom exceeding 4 cm as an adult. The bold eyespot on its tail base gives the species its common name. It requires soft, slightly acidic water, live micro-foods, and calm tankmates — making it a species best suited to experienced keepers.
careNotes: The Eyespot Gourami is one of the most rarely encountered anabantoids in the hobby, collected almost exclusively from the upper Ayeyarwady River drainage and the shores of Lake Indawgyi in Kachin State, northern Myanmar. Its natural habitat consists of shallow, muddy, slow-moving or still pools with dense emergent vegetation, leaf litter, and submerged branch tangles — conditions that should be replicated as closely as possible in captivity. A tank of at least 40 L with a 45 cm footprint is sufficient for a group, but larger setups improve stability and allow more naturalistic social dynamics. Soft, low-mineral water (gH 1–10, kH 1–5) is critical; the species is intolerant of hard or alkaline conditions for prolonged periods. The natural habitat experiences dramatic diel and seasonal temperature swings — daytime highs of 25–28°C and nocturnal lows approaching 15°C — and replicating a modest temperature cycle (e.g., 20–24°C daytime, cooler at night via natural room temperature) appears to support long-term health and reduce disease susceptibility. Stable, artificially constant tropical temperatures have been associated with chronic disease issues. Lighting should be subdued; floating plants and leaf litter help diffuse light and replicate the shaded biotope. Filtration should provide gentle flow only — a sponge filter is ideal. This species is NOT related to the Chocolate Gourami (Sphaerichthys osphromenoides), which belongs to a different genus; hobbyist confusion arises from superficial color similarity and shared Myanmar/Southeast Asian provenance. The conspicuous ocellus on the caudal peduncle is a reliable identification feature. Keep in groups of at least 6 — solitary specimens are shy and rarely seen; conspecific interaction encourages natural behavior and feeding responses. Do not mix with boisterous or significantly larger species. Due to extreme rarity in trade, source from specialist importers; wild-caught specimens may require extended live-food-only feeding periods before accepting frozen substitutes.
breedingNotes: Males construct small bubble nests at or near the water surface, often incorporating plant material or leaf debris. Unlike its closest relatives in Sphaerichthys — which are mouthbrooders, meaning the parent holds fertilised eggs in the mouth rather than in a nest — the Eyespot Gourami builds a bubblenest, though the eggs reportedly resemble those of mouthbrooding gouramis in appearance. Breeding is triggered by a short cooling period (dropping temperature to 15–18°C for several weeks) followed by a gradual raise back to 25°C, combined with slightly harder, more alkaline water (pH 7.5–8.5) and rich conditioning on live foods. Spawning occurs over several days with the male guarding the nest. Incubation takes approximately 3–5 days; fry become free-swimming shortly thereafter and require extremely fine foods — infusoria (microscopic single-celled organisms) and rotifers initially — before graduating to Artemia nauplii. Overall breeding difficulty is high, and even experienced keepers report mixed success.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Parasphaerichthys ocellatus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided native range (upper Ayeyarwady, Myitkyina, Lake Indawgyi), temperature 15–25°C, pH 6.5–7.5, hardness 90–179 ppm, tank minimum 45×30 cm, diet (micropredator; Artemia nauplii, Daphnia, grindal worms), group size ≥6, breeding behavior (bubble nest, 3–5 day incubation), labyrinth organ, and maximum SL 32 mm.

additional.name: FishBase — Parasphaerichthys ocellatus
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Osphronemidae, Anabantiformes, subfamily Luciocephalinae), maximum length 5.0 cm TL, native range (Myanmar), habitat (small muddy streams, benthopelagic, facultative air-breather), IUCN Least Concern, harmless to humans, described by Prashad & Mukerji 1929.

additional.name: Aquarium Glaser — Parasphaerichthys ocellatus
additional.notes: Trade importer profile. Noted extreme rarity in import, small size (2–3 cm in trade specimens), dramatic natural diel temperature swings (15°C nights to 25–28°C days), bubblenest breeding despite relationship to mouthbrooding Sphaerichthys, and risk of disease under stable constant temperatures. Confirmed 'Ocellated dwarf chocolate gourami' as alternate name.

additional.name: Biotope Aquarium Project — Indawgyi Lake, Kachin State, Myanmar
additional.notes: Field biotope data for Lake Indawgyi, one of the primary collection localities for this species. Recorded pH 7.0, GH ~7 mg/L (very soft), temperature 21–31°C seasonal range, slow current, medium transparency. Confirms extremely soft, near-neutral water conditions in the wild.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Parasphaerichthys ocellatus
additional.notes: Hobbyist magazine profile; confirmed aquarium care overview including group-keeping requirement (≥6 individuals), shy retiring temperament, small peaceful tankmate recommendations (small cyprinids, diminutive loaches), and advanced care classification. Site returned HTTP 403 on direct fetch; data cross-confirmed via search snippet and SeriouslyFish.

fish.breedingNotes: The Eyespot Gourami is a bubblenest builder that requires a deliberate cooling trigger — dropping the temperature to 15–18°C for several weeks — followed by a gradual rise back to around 25°C, combined with slightly harder and more alkaline water (pH 7.5–8.5) and intensive conditioning on live foods. Incubation lasts approximately 3–5 days, after which the fry require infusoria and rotifers before they are large enough to accept Artemia nauplii. Captive breeding is rarely achieved.
